title: Medieval Times
objective: Students will explore medieval times concepts including Battle of Hastings and 1066, Medieval Pyramid of Power, The Medieval Church and more.

steps.content: Welcome to an exciting lesson about Battle of Hastings and 1066!
The events of 1066: the death of Edward the Confessor, three claimants to the throne, the Battle of Hastings, William the Conqueror, and the Bayeux Tapestry as a historical source
Let me show you something cool. Explain why there was a crisis when Edward the Confessor died in 1066
Could you tell you what happened in 1066, why it mattered, and what the Bayeux Tapestry shows us about those events?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Battle of Hastings and 1066: Explain why there was a crisis when Edward the Confessor died in 1066 Describe the Battle of Hastings and its outcome
Let's explore Medieval Pyramid of Power! Have you ever wondered about this?
How medieval society was organised: king at the top, then lords, then knights, then peasants/serfs; who owed what to whom; the pyramid of power and mutual obligations
Let me show you something cool. Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants
Could you explain the medieval class system — who was at the top, who was at the bottom, and what each group had to do for the others?

Great job! Today we learned about Medieval Pyramid of Power. Draw or describe the feudal pyramid showing king, lords, knights, and peasants Explain what each level owed to the level above (loyalty, military service, labour)
Let's explore The Medieval Church! Have you ever wondered about this?
The enormous power of the medieval Church: monasteries and the daily life of monks and nuns; building great cathedrals; pilgrimage as a religious journey; the Church's influence over everyday life
Let me show you something cool. Explain why the Church was so powerful in medieval times (owned land, controlled education, people feared God's punishment)
Could you explain why the Church was such a big part of everyone's life in medieval times and what monks and nuns actually did all day?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about The Medieval Church. Explain why the Church was so powerful in medieval times (owned land, controlled education, people feared God's punishment) Describe what daily life was like for a monk or nun
Let's explore The Crusades! Have you ever wondered about this?
A simplified account of the Crusades: why Europeans travelled to the Holy Land, what they found there, the cultural exchange between Christian Europe and the Islamic world
Let's practice together! Explain in simple terms why European armies travelled to the Holy Land
Could you explain what the Crusades were and describe something that Europeans learned about the wider world through these journeys?

You did it! Here's what we explored about The Crusades: Explain in simple terms why European armies travelled to the Holy Land Describe at least one thing Europeans learned from the Islamic world during the Crusades
Let's explore Castle Design Through the Ages! Have you ever wondered about this?
How castles were built and evolved: from wooden motte-and-bailey to stone keeps to concentric castles; rooms and their uses; how castle design responded to new attack methods
Let's practice together! Describe the difference between an early motte-and-bailey castle and a later stone castle
Could you explain how castles changed over time and describe what different rooms inside a castle were used for?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Castle Design Through the Ages: Describe the difference between an early motte-and-bailey castle and a later stone castle Name at least three rooms or areas in a medieval castle and their purpose
Let's explore Anglo-Saxon Britain! Have you ever wondered about this?
Who the Anglo-Saxons were: Germanic peoples who settled in Britain after the Romans left; their kingdoms, villages, place names, art, and eventual conversion to Christianity
Time to try this out: Explain who the Anglo-Saxons were and when they came to Britain (after the Romans left)
Could you explain who the Anglo-Saxons were and point out that many English place names come from Anglo-Saxon words?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Anglo-Saxon Britain: Explain who the Anglo-Saxons were and when they came to Britain (after the Romans left) Name at least two Anglo-Saxon kingdoms (e.g. Wessex, Mercia, Northumbria)
Let's discover the amazing world of Siege Warfare!
How castles were attacked and defended: siege weapons (trebuchets, battering rams, siege towers), boiling liquids, arrow slits, murder holes; the drama of a medieval siege
Time to try this out: Name at least three siege weapons or attack methods
If you was reading about a medieval siege, could they explain the weapons used to attack a castle and the clever ways defenders fought back?
Great job! Today we learned about Siege Warfare. Name at least three siege weapons or attack methods Describe at least two ways a castle was designed to resist attack
Today we're going to learn about The Black Death. Get ready for an adventure!
The Black Death of 1348-49: what the plague was, how it spread, its devastating death toll; how it changed society by giving surviving workers more power and higher wages
Let me show you something cool. Describe what the Black Death was and at least two symptoms
Could you tell you what the Black Death was, how it spread, and why it actually ended up changing society in unexpected ways?
You did it! Here's what we explored about The Black Death: Describe what the Black Death was and at least two symptoms Explain how the plague spread (fleas on rats, person to person)
Have you ever thought about Vikings vs Anglo-Saxons? Let's find out together!
The conflict between Vikings and Anglo-Saxons for control of England: Viking raids, Alfred the Great's resistance, the Danelaw, Athelstan as first king of all England, Edward the Confessor
Let me show you something cool. Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact
Could you tell you about the struggle between the Vikings and Anglo-Saxons for control of England, and who Alfred the Great was?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Vikings vs Anglo-Saxons. Describe the Viking raids on Anglo-Saxon England and their impact Explain who Alfred the Great was and why he was important
